search_queries_used: site:bsg.org.uk acute lower gastrointestinal bleeding guideline initial assessment rectal bleeding
site:nice.org.uk NG12 rectal bleeding colorectal symptoms
candidate_sources_rejected: rectal bleeding automatically attributed to haemorrhoids or cancer
automatic stability score, colonoscopy, imaging, transfusion, medicine change, admission, or referral
automatic diagnosis, investigation, treatment, medication, dose, procedure, referral, escalation, or discharge instructions
search snippets, landing pages, and broad source-family matches without an exact applicable section
rejection_reasons: Bleeding source and severity require direct assessment.
No investigation, treatment, or disposition is generated.
Autonomous clinical output is outside this documentation-only research programme and was not mapped.
Only opened exact official documents and directly applicable sections were accepted as evidence.
population_applicability: Adults with lower-GI or rectal bleeding; children, pregnancy, postpartum, anticoagulation, known IBD, trauma, postoperative, and unstable bleeding require tailored pathways.
setting_applicability: Primary, gastroenterology, hepatology, colorectal, result-review, or outpatient follow-up as qualified by the exact source.
UAE_applicability: International evidence requires UAE gastroenterology, colorectal, hepatology, laboratory, endoscopy, referral, prescribing, screening, and local-pathway adaptation.
recency_verification: Exact official documents and sections were reviewed on 2026-07-14.
superseded_check: The cited official source remains current on the issuing organisation website at the review date.

evidence_items.direct_relationship: The exact lower-GI bleeding section supports bleeding amount and character, stool association, onset, recurrence, associated symptoms, and presentation context without assigning source or severity.
evidence_items.paraphrased_evidence_summary: Defines acute adult hospital lower gastrointestinal bleeding as bright or dark red blood per rectum, clots per rectum, or blood mixed with stool, while warning that brisk bleeding should not automatically be ascribed to haemorrhoids.

evidence_items.direct_relationship: The exact initial-assessment section supports clinician-entered vital signs, general and abdominal assessment, and concern documentation without calculating a score or asserting stability.
evidence_items.paraphrased_evidence_summary: Supports routine observations, full history and examination including digital rectal examination, appropriate blood tests, heart rate, systolic blood pressure, haemoglobin, and previous lower-GI-bleeding admission context.

evidence_items.direct_relationship: The exact section supports recording anticoagulant or antiplatelet context and already reviewed results without changing medication.
evidence_items.paraphrased_evidence_summary: Supports documenting whether anticoagulant or antiplatelet treatment is relevant to acute lower gastrointestinal bleeding; medicine interruption, reversal, and restart instructions are deliberately not mapped.

evidence_items.direct_relationship: The exact stability and follow-up section supports clinician-entered disposition, safety net, and follow-up documentation without assigning urgency.
evidence_items.paraphrased_evidence_summary: Supports clinician assessment of haemodynamic stability, major versus minor bleeding context, and qualified clinician-decided hospital or outpatient follow-up documentation without calculating a score or inserting disposition advice.

unresolved_source_gaps: Bleeding amount, haemodynamics, anaemia, anticoagulation, anorectal findings, consent, chaperone, source, diagnosis, tests, treatment, urgency, and disposition remain unsupported.
Any legacy item not explicitly mapped to an exact section remains unsupported pending qualified clinician review.
No mapping authorises an automatic diagnosis, differential, investigation, management decision, medicine, dose, procedure, referral, urgency conclusion, discharge decision, or patient instruction.
